From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Category of areas of England

Not to be confused with the [Ceremonial counties of England](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") or [Home counties](/wiki/Home_counties "Home counties").

See also: [Counties of England](/wiki/Counties_of_England "Counties of England")

The **historic counties of England** were [subdivisions](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_England "Subdivisions of England") that were established for administration by the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans"), in many cases based on earlier [kingdoms](/wiki/Heptarchy "Heptarchy") and [shires](/wiki/Shire "Shire") created by the [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)"), [Saxons](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons"), [Jutes](/wiki/Jutes "Jutes"), [Celts](/wiki/Celts "Celts") and the [Danes](/wiki/Danes_(tribe) "Danes (tribe)") and [Norse](/wiki/Norsemen "Norsemen") in the North. They are alternatively known as **ancient counties**,[[2]](#cite_note-1891census-2)[[3]](#cite_note-youngsdef-3) **traditional counties**,[[4]](#cite_note-guide-4) *former counties*[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) or simply as **counties**.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In the centuries that followed their establishment, as well as their administrative function, the counties also helped define local culture and identity.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) This role continued even after the counties ceased to be used for administration after the creation of [administrative counties](/wiki/Administrative_counties_of_England "Administrative counties of England") in 1889,[[10]](#cite_note-10) which were themselves amended by further local government reforms in the years following.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Unlike the partly self-governing [boroughs](/wiki/Ancient_borough "Ancient borough") that covered urban areas, the counties of medieval England existed primarily as a means of enforcing central government power, enabling monarchs to exercise control over local areas through their chosen representatives – originally [sheriffs](/wiki/High_sheriff "High sheriff") and later the [lord-lieutenants](/wiki/Lord-lieutenant "Lord-lieutenant") – and their subordinate [justices of the peace](/wiki/Justice_of_the_peace "Justice of the peace").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Counties were used initially for the [administration of justice](/wiki/Administration_of_justice "Administration of justice"), collection of taxes and organisation of the military, and later for local government and electing parliamentary representation.[[14]](#cite_note-Hackwood-14)[[15]](#cite_note-bryne-15) They continue to form the basis of [modern local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England") areas in many parts of the country away from the main urban areas, although the newly created areas sometimes have considerably altered boundaries from the historic counties on which they are based.[[4]](#cite_note-guide-4)[[16]](#cite_note-aspects-16)[[17]](#cite_note-hampton-17)

[Compare with 2010](/wiki/Counties_of_England#Local_government "Counties of England")

The name of a county often gives a clue to how it was formed, either as a division that took its name from a centre of administration, an ancient kingdom, or an area occupied by an ethnic group.[[14]](#cite_note-Hackwood-14) The majority of English counties are in the first category, with the name formed by combining the central town with the suffix *-[shire](/wiki/Shire "Shire")*, for example [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire"). Counties that were originally Anglo-Saxon kingdoms did not feature this formulation (Kent, Sussex, Essex, Middlesex). Some of these names include compass directions. The third category includes counties such as [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") and [Devon](/wiki/Devon "Devon") where the name corresponds to the tribes that inhabited the area.[[14]](#cite_note-Hackwood-14) [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") is anomalous in terms of naming and origin, not falling into any of the three categories. Instead, it was a [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") that was turned into the [County Palatine of Durham](/wiki/County_Palatine_of_Durham "County Palatine of Durham"), ruled by the [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham").[[14]](#cite_note-Hackwood-14) The expected form would otherwise be *Durhamshire*, but it was rarely used.

There are customary abbreviations for many of the counties. In most cases, these consist of simple truncation, usually with an *s* at the end signifying *-shire*, such as *Berks* for [Berkshire](/wiki/Berkshire "Berkshire") or *Bucks* for [Buckinghamshire](/wiki/Buckinghamshire "Buckinghamshire"). Some abbreviations are not obvious, such as *Salop* for [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"), from the [Norman](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_England "Norman conquest of England")-derived word for its county town [Shrewsbury](/wiki/Shrewsbury "Shrewsbury"); *Oxon* for [Oxfordshire](/wiki/Oxfordshire "Oxfordshire"), from Latin *Oxonia* (referring to both the county and the city of [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford")); *Hants* for [Hampshire](/wiki/Hampshire "Hampshire"); and *Northants* for [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire").[[14]](#cite_note-Hackwood-14) Counties can be prefixed with *County of* in official contexts with any *-shire* suffix dropped, such as *County of Kent* or *County of York*. An exception is made for Berkshire, a county with a *-shire* suffix which is not named after a town and whose official title has been *Royal County of Berkshire* since 1967. The *-shire* suffix was also appended for some counties, such as *Devonshire*, *Dorsetshire* and *Somersetshire*, despite their origin.[[18]](#cite_note-18) For instance, there has been an [Earl of Devonshire](/wiki/Earl_of_Devonshire "Earl of Devonshire") since 1603, and [Duke of Devonshire](/wiki/Duke_of_Devonshire "Duke of Devonshire") since 1694.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Doomsday_Book_-_Counties_of_England_-_1086.png/250px-Doomsday_Book_-_Counties_of_England_-_1086.png)](/wiki/File:Doomsday_Book_-_Counties_of_England_-_1086.png)

The Counties of England as recorded in the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book")

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origins")]

[Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") was first divided into administrative areas by the Romans, most likely following major geographical features such as rivers.[[14]](#cite_note-Hackwood-14) Before their arrival there were distinct tribal areas, but they were in a constant state of flux as territory was gained and lost. After the demise of [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") around 410 these first divisions of land were generally abandoned, although traditional divisions taking the form of petty kingdoms such as [Powys](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Powys "Kingdom of Powys"), [Dumnonia](/wiki/Dumnonia "Dumnonia") and [Elmet](/wiki/Elmet "Elmet") remained in those areas which remained [British](/wiki/Britons_(historical) "Britons (historical)"), such as [south west England](/wiki/South_West_England "South West England"). The areas that would later form the English counties started to take shape soon afterwards, with the [Kingdom of Kent](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kent "Kingdom of Kent") founded by settlers around 445. In southern England more widely, [shires](/wiki/Shire "Shire") emerged from earlier sub-kingdoms as part of the administrative structure of [Wessex](/wiki/Wessex "Wessex"), which then imposed its system of shires, [boroughs](/wiki/Boroughs "Boroughs") (or *[burhs](/wiki/Burh "Burh")*) and *[ealdormen](/wiki/Ealdormen "Ealdormen")* on [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia") after it came under West Saxon control during the 10th century.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Once the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") was united as a whole in 927 it became necessary to subdivide it for administrative convenience and to this end, [earldoms](/wiki/Earl "Earl") were created out of the earlier kingdoms, which were in turn subdivided into shires. The whole kingdom was divided into shires by the time of the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"). [Robert of Gloucester](/wiki/Robert_of_Gloucester_(historian) "Robert of Gloucester (historian)") accounts for thirty-five shires and [William of Malmesbury](/wiki/William_of_Malmesbury "William of Malmesbury") thirty-two,[[14]](#cite_note-Hackwood-14) Henry of Huntingdon, thirty-seven.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In Anglo-Saxon times the [earl](/wiki/Earl "Earl") and sheriff were jointly responsible for administering each shire through its [shire court](/wiki/Shire_court "Shire court").[[21]](#cite_note-21) Each earl was responsible for multiple shires, with some fluctuation in which shires belonged to which earldoms during this period.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In the years following the Norman Conquest of 1066 the large earldoms of the Anglo-Saxon era were gradually replaced by smaller earldoms corresponding to a single shire.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The Norman French term for an earl was a *comte* or [count](/wiki/Count "Count"); whilst in England the title count was not used for the person, the territory they controlled nevertheless became known as a 'county'. As the shires and counties were generally the same areas from the 12th century onwards, the terms shire and county came to be used interchangeably. The earls' role in administering their counties was gradually reduced as the shire court was eclipsed in importance by other courts, notably the [assizes](/wiki/Assizes "Assizes") and [quarter sessions](/wiki/Quarter_sessions "Quarter sessions").[[14]](#cite_note-Hackwood-14) Later earldoms were created that were not named after a shire, particularly from the 17th century onwards, but by that time the title of earl was honorary, with no effective role in local administration.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Gray1824.englandwales.jpg/250px-Gray1824.englandwales.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gray1824.englandwales.jpg)

An 1824 map of the English and Welsh counties

Although all of England was divided into shires by the time of the Norman Conquest, some counties were formed later, such as Lancashire in the 12th century. Perhaps because of their differing origins the counties [varied considerably in size](/wiki/List_of_counties_of_England_by_area_in_1831 "List of counties of England by area in 1831"). The county boundaries were fairly static until the [Local Government Act 1888](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1888 "Local Government Act 1888").[[25]](#cite_note-vob_census-25) Each shire was responsible for gathering taxes for the central government; for local defence; and for justice, through [assize courts](/wiki/Assize_courts "Assize courts").[[26]](#cite_note-winchester-26)

#### Southern England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Southern England")]

In southern England the counties were mostly subdivisions of the Kingdom of [Wessex](/wiki/Wessex "Wessex"), and in many areas represented annexed, previously independent, kingdoms or other tribal territories. [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent") derives from the [Kingdom of Kent](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kent "Kingdom of Kent"), Surrey from the Anglian word for 'southern region',[[27]](#cite_note-27) and [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"), [Sussex](/wiki/Sussex "Sussex") and [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex") come from the [East Saxons](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Essex "Kingdom of Essex"), [South Saxons](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sussex "Kingdom of Sussex") and [Middle Saxons](/wiki/Middle_Saxons "Middle Saxons"). Norfolk and Suffolk were subdivisions representing the "North Folk" and "South Folk" of the [Kingdom of East Anglia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_East_Anglia "Kingdom of East Anglia"). Only one county on the south coast of England now usually takes the suffix "-shire": [Hampshire](/wiki/Hampshire "Hampshire"), named after the former town of "Hamwic" (sic), the site of which is now a part of the city of [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton"). A "lost" Saxon county was [Winchcombeshire](/wiki/Winchcombeshire "Winchcombeshire") which lasted from 1007 to 1017 before being incorporated into [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire").[[28]](#cite_note-28) [Dorset](/wiki/Dorset "Dorset") and [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset "Somerset") derive their names from the *saete* or inhabitants of the areas around the towns of [Dorchester](/wiki/Dorchester,_Dorset "Dorchester, Dorset") and [Somerton](/wiki/Somerton,_Somerset "Somerton, Somerset") respectively; the names were first used by the Saxons in the 9th century.[[29]](#cite_note-29) [Devon](/wiki/Devon "Devon") and [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") were based on the pre-Saxon [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_Britain "Celtic Britain") tribes known in Latin as the [Dumnonii](/wiki/Dumnonii "Dumnonii") and [Cornovii](/wiki/Cornovii_(Cornwall) "Cornovii (Cornwall)"), in the latter case with the suffix *[wealas](/wiki/*Walhaz "*Walhaz")*, meaning foreigners, added by the Saxons.

#### Midlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Midlands")]

When Wessex annexed [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia") in the 10th century, it subdivided the area into various shires of roughly equal size and tax-raising potential or [hidage](/wiki/Hide_(unit) "Hide (unit)"). These generally took the name of the main town (the [county town](/wiki/County_town "County town")) of the county, along with "-shire". Examples are [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire") and [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire"). In some cases the original names have been worn down: for example, [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire "Cheshire") was originally "Chestershire".[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In the east Midlands, it is thought that county boundaries may represent a 9th-century division of the [Danelaw](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") between units of the Danish army.[[26]](#cite_note-winchester-26) The [soke](/wiki/Soke_(legal) "Soke (legal)") of Rutland was originally an exclave of [Nottinghamshire](/wiki/Nottinghamshire "Nottinghamshire"), but it eventually became the smallest county. [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire") was the successor to the [Kingdom of Lindsey](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Lindsey "Kingdom of Lindsey"), and took on the territories of [Kesteven](/wiki/Kesteven "Kesteven") and [Holland](/wiki/Holland,_Lincolnshire "Holland, Lincolnshire") when [Stamford](/wiki/Stamford,_Lincolnshire "Stamford, Lincolnshire") became the only Danelaw borough to fail to become a county town.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

#### Northern England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Northern England")]

Much of Northumbria was also shired, the best known of these counties being [Hallamshire](/wiki/Hallamshire "Hallamshire") and [Cravenshire](/wiki/Cravenshire "Cravenshire"). The Normans did not use these divisions, and so they are not generally regarded as ancient counties. The huge county of Yorkshire was a successor to the Viking [kingdom of York](/wiki/Kingdom_of_York "Kingdom of York") and the [Brittonic](/wiki/Celtic_Britons "Celtic Britons") kingdom of [Elmet](/wiki/Elmet "Elmet"); at the time of the Domesday Book in 1086 it included large areas of what would later become [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire"), [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland "Cumberland") and [Westmorland](/wiki/Westmorland "Westmorland"); most of Cumberland and Westmorland were under [Scottish](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") rule until 1092. After the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") in 1066 and the [harrying of the North](/wiki/Harrying_of_the_North "Harrying of the North"), much of the North of England was left depopulated and was included in the returns for [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire "Cheshire") and [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire") in the Domesday Book.[[32]](#cite_note-32) However, there is some disagreement about the status of some of this land. The area in between the [River Ribble](/wiki/River_Ribble "River Ribble") and the River [Mersey](/wiki/Mersey "Mersey"), referred to as *"Inter Ripam et Mersam"* in the Domesday Book,[[33]](#cite_note-sylvesterp14-33) was included in the returns for Cheshire.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Whether this meant that this land was actually part of Cheshire is however not clear.[[33]](#cite_note-sylvesterp14-33)[[35]](#cite_note-handt-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-crosby-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) The Northeast land that later became [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") and [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), was left unrecorded.

Cumberland, Westmorland, Lancashire, County Durham, and Northumberland were established as counties in the 12th century. Lancashire can be firmly dated to 1182.[[39]](#cite_note-George_D-39) Part of the domain of the [Bishops of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham"), [Hexhamshire](/wiki/Hexhamshire "Hexhamshire") was split off and was considered an independent county until 1572, when it became part of Northumberland.

### Welsh border

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Welsh border")]

At the time of the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book"), some parts of what later became [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") were included in English counties: [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth "Monmouth"), for example, was included in [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire").[[40]](#cite_note-40) Additionally, the Domesday Book included, as part of Cheshire, areas that later became part of Wales, including the two hundreds of [Atiscross and Exestan](/wiki/Hundreds_of_Cheshire "Hundreds of Cheshire"), and the southern part of Duddestan Hundred (as it was known as the time), which later became known as [Maelor Saesneg](/wiki/Maelor_Saesneg "Maelor Saesneg") (English Maelor), and (later still) "[Flintshire](/wiki/Flintshire_(historic) "Flintshire (historic)") Detached".[[41]](#cite_note-41) Parts of the [March of Wales](/wiki/Welsh_Marches "Welsh Marches"), which after the [Norman conquest](/wiki/Norman_conquest "Norman conquest") had been administered by [Marcher Lords](/wiki/Marcher_Lord "Marcher Lord") largely independently of the English monarch, were incorporated into the English counties of Shropshire, Herefordshire and Gloucestershire in 1535.

There was historic ambiguity as to the status of the county of [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire_(historic) "Monmouthshire (historic)"). As with other Marcher areas added to existing counties, it was created out of "the said Country or Dominion of Wales" by the [Laws in Wales Act 1535](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535_and_1542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542"). It was then added to the [Oxford circuit](/wiki/Oxford_circuit "Oxford circuit") of the English Assizes. For legal purposes it was regarded as part of England, but Laws since the late 19th century were often applied to "[Wales and Monmouthshire](/wiki/Wales_and_Monmouthshire "Wales and Monmouthshire")".[[42]](#cite_note-42) It was listed among the English counties for parliamentary purposes until 1950 and for local government until 1974, but the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972") unambiguously included the area as part of Wales.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Counties corporate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Counties corporate")]

Main article: [County corporate](/wiki/County_corporate "County corporate")

A [charter](/wiki/Charter "Charter") of [Henry I](/wiki/Henry_I_of_England "Henry I of England") in about 1130 gave the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") its own [Sheriff](/wiki/Sheriffs_of_the_City_of_London "Sheriffs of the City of London").[[44]](#cite_note-44) The Sheriff of London also had jurisdiction over the county of Middlesex, so that "London and Middlesex were from that time regarded as one from an administrative point of view",[[45]](#cite_note-vch_middlesex-45) although they retained their separate identities. This relationship continued until the [Local Government Act 1888](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1888 "Local Government Act 1888") created a new office of [High Sheriff of Middlesex](/wiki/High_Sheriff_of_Middlesex "High Sheriff of Middlesex") appointed in the same manner as other English and Welsh counties, created the [County of London](/wiki/County_of_London "County of London") with its own high sheriff, and restricted the jurisdiction of the sheriffs of London to the City.[[45]](#cite_note-vch_middlesex-45)

During the Middle Ages a number of other large cities and towns were granted the status of self-governing counties separate from adjacent counties. Such a county became known as a [county corporate](/wiki/County_corporate "County corporate") or "county of itself". For most practical purposes this separate status was replaced in the late 19th century when [county boroughs](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") were introduced.

[Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol") developed as a major port in the medieval period, straddling both sides of the [River Avon](/wiki/River_Avon_(Bristol) "River Avon (Bristol)") which formed the ancient boundary between [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire") and [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset "Somerset"). In 1373 [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England") decreed

> …that the said town of Bristol with its suburbs and their precinct, as the boundaries now exist, henceforward shall be separated and exempt in every way from the said counties of Gloucester and Somerset, on land and by water; that it shall be a county in itself and be called the county of Bristol for ever…[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Similar arrangements were later applied to [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich") (1404), [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton") (1447), [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury "Canterbury") (1471), [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester "Gloucester") (1483), [Exeter](/wiki/Exeter "Exeter") (1537), and [Poole](/wiki/Poole "Poole") (1571).[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Charters were granted constituting the boroughs or cities of [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln,_Lincolnshire "Lincoln, Lincolnshire") (1409), [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham") (1448), [Lichfield](/wiki/Lichfield "Lichfield") (1556) and [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester,_England "Worcester, England") (1622) as counties. The [County of the City of Coventry](/wiki/County_of_the_City_of_Coventry "County of the City of Coventry") was separated from Warwickshire in 1451, and included an extensive area of countryside surrounding the city.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Charters granting separate county status to the cities and boroughs of [Chester](/wiki/Chester "Chester") (1238/9), [York](/wiki/York "York") (1396), [Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne") (1400) and [Kingston-upon-Hull](/wiki/Kingston-upon-Hull "Kingston-upon-Hull") (with the surrounding area of [Hullshire](/wiki/Hullshire "Hullshire")) (1440). In 1551 [Berwick upon Tweed](/wiki/Berwick_upon_Tweed "Berwick upon Tweed"), on the border with [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), was created a county corporate.

### Exclaves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Exclaves")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/68/DudleyTraditionalDetail.jpg)](/wiki/File:DudleyTraditionalDetail.jpg)

This 1814 map shows [Dudley](/wiki/Dudley "Dudley") in a [detached part](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") of [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") surrounded by [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"), an exclave of [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire") (the parish of [Halesowen](/wiki/Halesowen "Halesowen")) to the south-east, and part of Staffordshire ([Broome](/wiki/Broome,_Worcestershire "Broome, Worcestershire") and [Clent](/wiki/Clent "Clent")) to the south-west

The ancient counties have many anomalies, and many small [exclaves](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave"), where a parcel of land was politically part of one county despite not being physically connected to the rest of the county. The [Counties (Detached Parts) Act 1844](/wiki/Counties_(Detached_Parts)_Act_1844 "Counties (Detached Parts) Act 1844") was passed, the effect of which was to treat many of these exclaves as part of the county which surrounded them. This had already been done for Parliamentary purposes under the [Great Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832").

Large exclaves affected by the 1844 Act included the County Durham exclaves of [Islandshire](/wiki/Islandshire "Islandshire"), [Bedlingtonshire](/wiki/Bedlingtonshire "Bedlingtonshire") and [Norhamshire](/wiki/Norhamshire "Norhamshire"), which were subsequently treated as hundreds of [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"); and those parts of [Halesowen](/wiki/Halesowen_(ancient_parish) "Halesowen (ancient parish)") forming part of Shropshire, which was [subsequently treated](/wiki/Evolution_of_Worcestershire_county_boundaries_since_1844 "Evolution of Worcestershire county boundaries since 1844") as part of [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire"), as the remainder already was.

[Exclaves that the 1844 Act did not touch](/wiki/List_of_county_exclaves_in_England_and_Wales_1844_-_1974 "List of county exclaves in England and Wales 1844 - 1974") included the part of [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire") around [Donisthorpe](/wiki/Donisthorpe "Donisthorpe"), locally in [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire"); a part of [Huntingdonshire](/wiki/Huntingdonshire "Huntingdonshire") near [Woodbury Park](/wiki/Everton,_Bedfordshire#Woodbury_Park "Everton, Bedfordshire"), separated by [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire"); and most of the larger exclaves of Worcestershire, including the town of [Dudley](/wiki/Dudley "Dudley"), which remained surrounded by [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"). Additionally, the [Furness](/wiki/Furness "Furness") portion of [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire") remained separated from the rest of Lancashire by a narrow strip of Westmorland — though it was accessible by way of the [Morecambe Bay](/wiki/Morecambe_Bay "Morecambe Bay") [tidal flats](/wiki/Mudflat "Mudflat").

### 1889

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1889")]

When the first county councils were set up in 1889, they covered newly created entities known as [administrative counties](/wiki/Administrative_counties_of_England "Administrative counties of England"). Several historic subdivisions with separate county administrations were also created administrative counties, particularly the separate [ridings](/wiki/Riding_(division) "Riding (division)") of [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire"), the separate [parts](/wiki/Parts_of_Lincolnshire "Parts of Lincolnshire") of Lincolnshire, and the East and West divisions of Sussex.[[49]](#cite_note-49) The [Local Government Act 1888](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1888 "Local Government Act 1888") also contained wording to create both a new "administrative county" and a "county" of [London](/wiki/County_of_London "County of London"),[[50]](#cite_note-50) and to ensure that the [county boroughs](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") which were created at the same time continued for non-administrative purposes to be part of the county which they geographically lay.[[51]](#cite_note-51) These counties were to be used "for all purposes, whether sheriff, lieutenant, custos rotulorum, justices, militia, coroner, or other". The effect was that new county boroughs which were counties corporate retained their status as separate counties. In retrospect, these "statutory" counties can be identified as the predecessors of the ceremonial counties of England. The censuses of 1891, 1901 and 1911 provided figures for the "ancient counties".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Corn_Exchange_%283622709872%29.jpg/250px-Corn_Exchange_%283622709872%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corn_Exchange_(3622709872).jpg)

Notice on the Corn Exchange, [Royal Tunbridge Wells](/wiki/Royal_Tunbridge_Wells "Royal Tunbridge Wells"), mentioning the historic county boundary

Several towns are historically divided between counties, including [Banbury](/wiki/Banbury "Banbury"), [Burton upon Trent](/wiki/Burton_upon_Trent "Burton upon Trent"), [Newmarket](/wiki/Newmarket,_Suffolk "Newmarket, Suffolk"), [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough "Peterborough"), [Royal Tunbridge Wells](/wiki/Royal_Tunbridge_Wells "Royal Tunbridge Wells"), [Royston](/wiki/Royston,_Hertfordshire "Royston, Hertfordshire"), Stamford, [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth,_Staffordshire "Tamworth, Staffordshire"), [Todmorden](/wiki/Todmorden "Todmorden"), [Warrington](/wiki/Warrington "Warrington") and [Wisbech](/wiki/Wisbech "Wisbech"). In Newmarket and Tamworth the historic county boundary runs right up the middle of the high street; in Royal Tunbridge Wells the historic county boundary had a theatre (now the Corn Exchange) built right on it, with the actors playing in Sussex to an audience in Kent; and in Todmorden, the historically fractious border between Lancashire and Yorkshire (the river known as Walsden Water) had [Todmorden Town Hall](/wiki/Todmorden_Town_Hall "Todmorden Town Hall") built right on top of it on a [culvert](/wiki/Culvert "Culvert") tunnel, dividing the hall down the middle between the two counties – a division reflected in its architecture. The 1888 Act ensured that every urban sanitary district would be considered to be part of a single county. This principle was maintained in the 20th century: when county boroughs such as [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), [Reading](/wiki/Reading,_Berkshire "Reading, Berkshire") and [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield") expanded into neighbouring counties, the area added became associated with the county borough's geographic county. This principle was not, however, applied to [Stockport](/wiki/Stockport "Stockport")[[52]](#cite_note-52) or [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"), which remained divided, the latter even divided between Wales and England[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) (from 1938 Cardiff included [Rumney](/wiki/Rumney,_Cardiff "Rumney, Cardiff") in the territory of the historic county of [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire "Monmouthshire"), which was legally regarded as part of England until 1972 when it was instead assigned to Wales).

### 1965 and 1974

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1965 and 1974")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Warwickshiremap_700.jpg/250px-Warwickshiremap_700.jpg)](/wiki/File:Warwickshiremap_700.jpg)

The ancient county boundaries of [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire") cover a larger area than the administrative area in 1974 (in green).

On 1 April 1965, a number of changes came into effect. The new administrative area of [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") was created, resulting in the abolition of the administrative counties of London and Middlesex, at the same time taking in areas from surrounding counties. On the same date the new counties of [Cambridgeshire and Isle of Ely](/wiki/Cambridgeshire_and_Isle_of_Ely "Cambridgeshire and Isle of Ely") and of [Huntingdon and Peterborough](/wiki/Huntingdon_and_Peterborough "Huntingdon and Peterborough") were formed by the merger of pairs of administrative counties. The new areas were also adopted for lieutenancy and shrievalty purposes.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)

In 1974 a major local government reform took place under the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"). The Act abolished administrative counties and county boroughs, and divided England (except Greater London and the Isles of Scilly) into counties. These were of two types: "metropolitan" and "non-metropolitan" counties.[[4]](#cite_note-guide-4)[[57]](#cite_note-57) Apart from local government, the new counties were "substituted for counties of any other description" for judicial, shrievalty, lieutenancy and other purposes.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Several counties, such as [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland "Cumberland"), [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire"), [Rutland](/wiki/Rutland "Rutland"), [Westmorland](/wiki/Westmorland "Westmorland") and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire"), vanished from the administrative map, while new entities such as [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(county) "Avon (county)"), [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland_(county) "Cleveland (county)"), [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria") and [Humberside](/wiki/Humberside "Humberside") appeared, in addition to the six new [metropolitan counties](/wiki/Metropolitan_county "Metropolitan county").[[4]](#cite_note-guide-4)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

The built-up areas of conurbations tend to cross historic county boundaries freely.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Examples are [Bournemouth](/wiki/Bournemouth "Bournemouth")–[Poole](/wiki/Poole "Poole")–[Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch,_Dorset "Christchurch, Dorset") ([Dorset](/wiki/Dorset "Dorset") and [Hampshire](/wiki/Hampshire "Hampshire")) [Greater Manchester](/wiki/Greater_Manchester "Greater Manchester") ([Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire "Cheshire"), [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire"), Yorkshire and [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire")), [Merseyside](/wiki/Merseyside "Merseyside") (Cheshire and Lancashire), [Teesside](/wiki/Teesside "Teesside") (Yorkshire and County Durham), [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire") (Yorkshire, [Nottinghamshire](/wiki/Nottinghamshire "Nottinghamshire") and [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire")), [Tyneside](/wiki/Tyneside "Tyneside") (County Durham and [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland")) and [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") ([Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"), [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire") and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire")). [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") itself straddles five ancient counties — [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"), [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire "Hertfordshire"), Kent, [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex"), [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey "Surrey") — and the London urban area sprawls into Buckinghamshire and Berkshire. The Local Government Act 1972 sought generally to unite conurbations within a single county, while retaining the historic county boundaries as far as was practicable.[[4]](#cite_note-guide-4)[[17]](#cite_note-hampton-17)

### Postal counties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Postal counties")]

Main article: [Postal counties of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Postal_counties_of_the_United_Kingdom "Postal counties of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Former_postal_counties_of_England.svg/250px-Former_postal_counties_of_England.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Former_postal_counties_of_England.svg)

Former postal counties of England from 1974 to 1996

In a period of financial crisis,[[61]](#cite_note-61) the [Post Office](/wiki/Royal_Mail "Royal Mail") was able to alter many of its [postal counties](/wiki/Postal_counties_of_the_United_Kingdom "Postal counties of the United Kingdom") in accordance with the 1965 and 1974 reforms, but not all. The two major exceptions were [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") and [Greater Manchester](/wiki/Greater_Manchester "Greater Manchester"). Greater London was not adopted in 1965, since, according to the Post Office at the time, it would have been too expensive to do so, while it gave as its reason for not adopting Greater Manchester the ambiguity of the name with the [Manchester post town](/wiki/M_postal_area "M postal area"). Perhaps as a result of this, the ancient counties appear not to have fallen completely out of use for locating places in Greater Manchester, along with areas of Greater London that are not part of the [London](/wiki/London_postal_district "London postal district") post town. It is common for people to speak of "[Uxbridge](/wiki/Uxbridge "Uxbridge"), Middlesex", "[Dagenham](/wiki/Dagenham "Dagenham"), Essex" or "[Bromley](/wiki/Bromley "Bromley"), Kent" (which are outside the London postal district), but much less so to speak of "[Brixton](/wiki/Brixton "Brixton"), Surrey", "[Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich "Greenwich"), Kent", or "[West Ham](/wiki/West_Ham "West Ham"), Essex" (which are inside it).

In 1996, following further local government reform and the modernisation of its sorting equipment, the Royal Mail ceased to use counties at all in the direction of mail.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Instead it now uses the outward code (first half) of the postcode. The former postal counties were removed in 2000 from its [Postcode Address File](/wiki/Postcode_Address_File "Postcode Address File") database and included in an "alias file",[[63]](#cite_note-63) which is used to [cross-reference](/wiki/Cross-reference "Cross-reference") details that may be added by users but are no longer required, such as former street names or historic, administrative and former postal counties.

During a public consultation in 2009 [Postcomm](/wiki/Postal_Services_Commission "Postal Services Commission") found that many respondents objected to the use of counties in the alias file. In May 2010 Postcomm announced that it was encouraging Royal Mail to discontinue the use of counties in its alias file at the earliest opportunity. However, because some existing software included the use of counties, Royal Mail was advised not to implement the change before 2013.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### County cricket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: County cricket")]

The historic counties of England continue to be used as the basis for [county cricket](/wiki/County_cricket "County cricket") teams[[65]](#cite_note-bbc-65) and the governance of [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") in England through the [ECB County Boards](/wiki/England_and_Wales_Cricket_Board#The_County_Boards "England and Wales Cricket Board").[[66]](#cite_note-66) There are exceptions in that Rutland is integrated with Leicestershire; the Isle of Wight has its own board outside the Hampshire one; there is a board for the ceremonial county of Cumbria which is representative of both Cumberland and Westmorland. In addition, the ECB County Boards include one for the country of Wales.

### Recognition of historic county boundaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Recognition of historic county boundaries")]

A review of the structure of local government in England by the [Local Government Commission for England](/wiki/Local_Government_Commission_for_England_(1992) "Local Government Commission for England (1992)") led to the restoration of the [East Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/East_Riding_of_Yorkshire "East Riding of Yorkshire"), [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire"), [Rutland](/wiki/Rutland "Rutland") and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") as administrative areas in the 1990s; the abolition of Avon, Cleveland and Humberside within 25 years of their creation; and the restoration of the traditional borders between Somerset and Gloucestershire (except at [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol")), County Durham and Yorkshire (towards the mouth of the [River Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees"); not in [Teesdale](/wiki/Teesdale "Teesdale")), and Yorkshire and Lincolnshire for ceremonial purposes in these areas. The case of Huntingdonshire was considered twice, but the Commission found that "there was no exceptional county allegiance to Huntingdonshire, as had been perceived in Rutland and Herefordshire".[[67]](#cite_note-cooksey-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Flag_of_County_Durham.svg/250px-Flag_of_County_Durham.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_County_Durham.svg)

The [flag of historic County Durham](/wiki/Flag_of_County_Durham "Flag of County Durham"), adopted in 2013 through a competition organised by the [Flag Institute](/wiki/Flag_Institute "Flag Institute")

The [Association of British Counties](/wiki/Association_of_British_Counties "Association of British Counties") (ABC), with its regional affiliates, such as the [Friends of Real Lancashire](/wiki/Friends_of_Real_Lancashire "Friends of Real Lancashire") and the [Yorkshire Ridings Society](/wiki/Yorkshire_Ridings_Society "Yorkshire Ridings Society"),[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) promotes the historic counties. It states that the *"...ABC contends that Britain needs a fixed popular geography, one divorced from the ever changing names and areas of local government...The ABC, therefore, seeks to fully re-establish the use of the historic counties as the standard popular geographical reference frame of Britain and to further encourage their use as a basis for social, sporting and cultural activities.*[[70]](#cite_note-70)

The Campaign for Historic Counties is dedicated to campaigning, both in the public arena and among parliamentarians, for the restoration of historic counties. Their objectives are:[[71]](#cite_note-71)

1. Maps, roads and addresses to included historic counties as standard
2. Removal of the word 'county' from all local council names
3. Historic Counties to be used for ceremonial purposes

In 2013, [Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Communities_and_Local_Government "Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government") [Eric Pickles](/wiki/Eric_Pickles "Eric Pickles") formally recognised and acknowledged the continued existence of England's 39 historic counties.[[72]](#cite_note-CLG-EP-72)[[73]](#cite_note-Ind-EP-73)[[74]](#cite_note-ABC-EP-74) On 23 April 2014 a new initiative was announced to support the 'tapestry' of traditional English counties, including the removal of a restriction preventing the names of traditional counties being displayed on street and road signs.[[75]](#cite_note-CLG-BG-75) In August 2014, the first road sign was erected to mark the boundary of the historic county of Yorkshire.[[76]](#cite_note-YP=YB-76) The Government is also publishing a new online interactive map of England's county boundaries.[[75]](#cite_note-CLG-BG-75) The Government has previously changed rules to allow local and county flags to be flown without planning permission, and supported the [Flag Institute](/wiki/Flag_Institute "Flag Institute") in encouraging a new wave of county and community flags to be designed and flown by local communities. The flags of England's historic counties have been flown from Government offices in support of these identities. All 39 counties have registered flags, with the flag of Leicestershire the last to be adopted. In July 2019 the UK Government published official guidance on Celebrating the Historic Counties of England, stating that
"the tapestry of England's historic counties is one of the bonds which draws our nation together".[[77]](#cite_note-77)

[Sussex](/wiki/Sussex "Sussex")[[78]](#cite_note-78) and [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire"),[[79]](#cite_note-special-79) both historic counties and long abandoned as units for administrative purposes, have continued to be widely recognised as cultural regions, significant in sport and used by many organisations as regional units. These counties, and several others, have a [county day](/wiki/List_of_county_days_in_England "List of county days in England") in which the culture and history of the historic county is celebrated; many of these county days were created in the 21st century.

A [direct action](/wiki/Direct_action "Direct action") group, [CountyWatch](/wiki/CountyWatch "CountyWatch"), was formed in 2004 to remove what its members consider to be wrongly placed county boundary signs that do not mark the historic or traditional county boundaries of England and [Wales](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_Wales "Historic counties of Wales"). They have removed, resorted or erected a number of what they claim to be "wrongly sited" county boundary signs in various parts of England. For instance, in Lancashire 30 signs were removed.[[80]](#cite_note-BBC_News4439228-80) CountyWatch has been criticised for such actions by the councils that erected the signs:[[81]](#cite_note-YP1199795-81) [Lancashire County Council](/wiki/Lancashire_County_Council "Lancashire County Council") pointed out that the taxpayers would have to pay for the signs to be re-erected.[[82]](#cite_note-BBC_News4436170-82)

The only political party with a manifesto commitment to restore the boundaries and political functions of all ancient counties, including [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex") and [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire_(historic) "Monmouthshire (historic)"), is the [English Democrats Party](/wiki/English_Democrats_Party "English Democrats Party").[[83]](#cite_note-edcounties-83)

## Functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Functions")]

By the late Middle Ages the county was being used as the basis of a number of functions.[[15]](#cite_note-bryne-15)

### Administration of justice and law enforcement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Administration of justice and law enforcement")]

Further information: [High sheriff](/wiki/High_sheriff "High sheriff")

At the head of the legal hierarchy were the [sheriff](/wiki/High_Sheriff "High Sheriff") and the [keeper of the rolls](/wiki/Custos_rotulorum "Custos rotulorum") for each county. A sheriff, a king-appointed official for the shire or "shire's [reeve](/wiki/Reeve_(England) "Reeve (England)")", held responsibility for keeping the peace. Some shires shared a sheriff with neighbouring counties for hundreds of years, such as:

* 1068-1566: [Nottinghamshire, Derbyshire and the Royal-Forests](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Nottinghamshire,_Derbyshire_and_the_Royal_Forests "Sheriff of Nottinghamshire, Derbyshire and the Royal Forests")
* 1125-1575: [Bedfordshire and Buckinghamshire](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Bedfordshire_and_Buckinghamshire "Sheriff of Bedfordshire and Buckinghamshire")
* 1154-1635: [Cambridgeshire and Huntingdonshire](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Cambridgeshire_and_Huntingdonshire "Sheriff of Cambridgeshire and Huntingdonshire")
* 1156-1567: [Essex and Hertfordshire](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Essex "Sheriff of Essex")
* 1158-1566: [Leicestershire](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Leicestershire "Sheriff of Leicestershire") and [Warwickshire](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Warwickshire "Sheriff of Warwickshire")
* 1204-1344: [Shropshire](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Shropshire "Sheriff of Shropshire") and [Staffordshire](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Staffordshire "Sheriff of Staffordshire")
* 1226-1567:  [Somerset and Dorset](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Somerset_and_Dorset "Sheriff of Somerset and Dorset")
* 1229-1635 (intermittent): [Surrey](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Surrey "Sheriff of Surrey") and [Sussex](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Sussex "Sheriff of Sussex")
* 1248-1566 [Berkshire and Oxfordshire](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Berkshire_and_Oxfordshire "Sheriff of Berkshire and Oxfordshire")
* before 1070-1576 [Norfolk and Suffolk](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Norfolk_and_Suffolk "Sheriff of Norfolk and Suffolk")

In 1908, the Lord Lieutenant became more senior than the Sheriff. The position of Sheriff also became more ceremonial: many shrieval responsibilities were transferred to High Court judges, magistrates, coroners, local authorities and the police.

The [Assize Courts](/wiki/Assize_Court "Assize Court") used counties, or their major divisions, as a basis for their organisation.[[16]](#cite_note-aspects-16) [Justices of the peace](/wiki/Justice_of_the_peace "Justice of the peace") originating in Norman times as Knights of the Peace,[[84]](#cite_note-elcock-84) were appointed in each county. Until the 19th century law enforcement was mostly carried out at the [parish](/wiki/Parish "Parish") level. With an increasingly mobile population, however, the system became outdated. Following the successful establishment of the [Metropolitan Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police") in London, the [County Police Act 1839](/wiki/County_Police_Act_1839 "County Police Act 1839") empowered justices of the peace to form county constabularies outside boroughs. The formation of county police forces was made compulsory by the [County and Borough Police Act 1856](/wiki/County_and_Borough_Police_Act_1856 "County and Borough Police Act 1856").

The justices had responsibility for maintaining county [gaols](/wiki/Gaol "Gaol") and [houses of correction](/wiki/House_of_correction "House of correction"). During the 19th century [penal reformers](/wiki/Prison_reform "Prison reform") campaigned against the often primitive conditions in gaols, and under the [Prison Act 1877](/wiki/Prison_Act_1877 "Prison Act 1877") they came under [Home Office](/wiki/Home_Office "Home Office") control.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

### Defence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Defence")]

In the 1540s the office of [Lord Lieutenant](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant "Lord Lieutenant") was instituted. The lieutenants had a military role, previously exercised by the sheriffs, and were made responsible for raising and organising the [militia](/wiki/Militia "Militia") in each county. The lieutenancies were subsequently given responsibility for the [Volunteer Force](/wiki/Volunteer_Force_(Great_Britain) "Volunteer Force (Great Britain)"). In 1871 the lieutenants lost their positions as heads of the militia, and their office became largely ceremonial.[[86]](#cite_note-86) The [Cardwell](/wiki/Cardwell_Reforms "Cardwell Reforms") and [Childers Reforms](/wiki/Childers_Reforms "Childers Reforms") of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") linked the recruiting areas of infantry regiments to the counties.

### Parliamentary representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Parliamentary representation")]

Each English county sent two [Knights of the Shire](/wiki/Knights_of_the_Shire "Knights of the Shire") to the [House of Commons](/wiki/British_House_of_Commons "British House of Commons") (in addition to the burgesses sent by boroughs). Yorkshire gained two members in 1821 when [Grampound](/wiki/Grampound_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Grampound (UK Parliament constituency)") was disenfranchised. The [Great Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832") reapportioned members throughout the counties, many of which were also split into parliamentary divisions. Constituencies based on the ancient county boundaries remained in use until 1918.

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Local government")]

From the 16th century onwards the county was increasingly used as a unit of [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") as the justices of the peace took on various administrative functions known as "county business". This was transacted at the [quarter sessions](/wiki/Quarter_sessions "Quarter sessions"), summoned four times a year by the lord lieutenant. By the 19th century the county magistrates were exercising powers over the licensing of alehouses, the construction of bridges, prisons and asylums, the superintendence of main roads, public buildings and charitable institutions, and the regulation of weights and measures.[[87]](#cite_note-87) The justices were empowered to levy local taxes to support these activities, and in 1739 these were unified as a single "county rate", under the control of a county treasurer.[[88]](#cite_note-88) In order to build and maintain roads and bridges, a salaried county surveyor was to be appointed.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

By the 1880s it was being suggested that it would be more efficient if a wider variety of functions were provided on a county-wide basis.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

### Subdivisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Subdivisions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Yorkshire_Ridings.png/250px-Yorkshire_Ridings.png)](/wiki/File:Yorkshire_Ridings.png)

Yorkshire has three major subdivisions known as the [ridings](/wiki/Riding_(division) "Riding (division)") of Yorkshire:

1. [North Riding](/wiki/North_Riding_of_Yorkshire "North Riding of Yorkshire")
2. [West Riding](/wiki/West_Riding_of_Yorkshire "West Riding of Yorkshire")
3. [East Riding](/wiki/East_Riding_of_Yorkshire "East Riding of Yorkshire")

Some of the counties had major subdivisions. Of these, the most significant were the divisions of Yorkshire: the [East Riding](/wiki/East_Riding_of_Yorkshire "East Riding of Yorkshire"), [West Riding](/wiki/West_Riding_of_Yorkshire "West Riding of Yorkshire"), [North Riding](/wiki/North_Riding_of_Yorkshire "North Riding of Yorkshire") and the [ainsty](/wiki/Ainsty "Ainsty") of York. Since Yorkshire was so large, its [ridings](/wiki/Riding_(division) "Riding (division)") became established as geographical terms quite apart from their original role as administrative divisions. The second largest county, [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire"), was also sub divided into three historic "[parts](/wiki/Parts_of_Lincolnshire "Parts of Lincolnshire")": [Parts of Lindsey](/wiki/Parts_of_Lindsey "Parts of Lindsey"), [Holland](/wiki/Holland,_Lincolnshire "Holland, Lincolnshire") and [Kesteven](/wiki/Kesteven "Kesteven"), and the Parts of Lindsey was itself divided into three ridings ([North Riding](/wiki/North_Riding_of_Lindsey "North Riding of Lindsey"), [South Riding](/wiki/South_Riding_of_Lindsey "South Riding of Lindsey") and [West Riding](/wiki/West_Riding_of_Lindsey "West Riding of Lindsey")). Other divisions include the special status of [Tower Hamlets](/wiki/Tower_division "Tower division") within [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex"), those of [Sussex](/wiki/Sussex "Sussex") into [East Sussex](/wiki/East_Sussex "East Sussex") and [West Sussex](/wiki/West_Sussex "West Sussex") and [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk") into [East Suffolk](/wiki/East_Suffolk_(county) "East Suffolk (county)") and [West Suffolk](/wiki/West_Suffolk_(county) "West Suffolk (county)"), and, more informally and hence more vaguely, of Kent into [East Kent](/wiki/East_Kent "East Kent") and [West Kent](/wiki/West_Kent "West Kent").

Several counties had [liberties](/wiki/Liberty_(division) "Liberty (division)") or [sokes](/wiki/Soke_(legal) "Soke (legal)") within them that were administered separately. [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire") had the [Isle of Ely](/wiki/Isle_of_Ely "Isle of Ely"), and [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire") had the [Soke of Peterborough](/wiki/Soke_of_Peterborough "Soke of Peterborough"). Such divisions were used by such entities as the [Quarter Sessions](/wiki/Quarter_Sessions "Quarter Sessions") courts and were inherited by the later [administrative county](/wiki/Administrative_county "Administrative county") areas under the control of [county councils](/wiki/County_council "County council").

Most English counties were subdivided into smaller subdivisions called [hundreds](/wiki/Hundred_(country_subdivision) "Hundred (country subdivision)"). Nottinghamshire, Yorkshire and Lincolnshire were divided into wapentakes (a unit of Danish origin), while Durham, Northumberland, Cumberland and Westmorland were divided into [wards](/wiki/Wards_of_the_United_Kingdom "Wards of the United Kingdom"), areas originally organised for military purposes, each centred on a castle.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Kent and Sussex had an intermediate level between the county and hundreds, known as [lathes](/wiki/Lathe_(county_subdivision) "Lathe (county subdivision)") in Kent and [rapes](/wiki/Rape_(county_subdivision) "Rape (county subdivision)") in Sussex. Hundreds or their equivalents were divided into [tithings](/wiki/Tithing_(country_subdivision) "Tithing (country subdivision)") and [parishes](/wiki/Parish "Parish") (the only class of these divisions still used administratively), which in turn were divided into [townships](/wiki/Township_(England) "Township (England)") and [manors](/wiki/Manorialism "Manorialism"). In the 17th century the [Ossulstone](/wiki/Ossulstone "Ossulstone") hundred of Middlesex was further divided into four divisions, which replaced the functions of the hundred. The borough and parish were the principal providers of local services throughout England until the creation of ad-hoc boards and, later, local government districts.

The historic counties of England are included in the [Index of Place Names](/wiki/Index_of_Place_Names "Index of Place Names") (IPN) published by the [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics"). Each "place" included in the IPN is related to the historic county it lies within, as well as to a set of administrative areas.

## Administrative and ceremonial uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Administrative and ceremonial uses")]

At the time of the [2009 structural changes to local government in England](/wiki/2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England "2009 structural changes to local government in England"), the ancient counties continue to form, with considerably altered boundaries, many of the [ceremonial](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") and [non-metropolitan counties](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") in England. Some ancient counties have their names preserved in multiple contemporary units, such as Yorkshire in [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire") and [West Yorkshire](/wiki/West_Yorkshire "West Yorkshire") or now correspond to another type of subdivision, such as the [Huntingdonshire](/wiki/Huntingdonshire "Huntingdonshire") district. In some areas ancient counties have been abandoned for local government use and then later revived.

## Other uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historic_counties_of_England&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Other uses")]

The [vice counties](/wiki/Watsonian_vice-counties "Watsonian vice-counties"), used for biological recording since 1852, are largely based on historic county boundaries. They ignore all exclaves and are modified by subdividing large counties and merging smaller areas into neighbouring counties; such as Rutland with Leicestershire and Furness with Westmorland. The static boundaries make [longitudinal study](/wiki/Longitudinal_study "Longitudinal study") of biodiversity easier. They also cover the rest of Great Britain and Ireland.
